[
  {
    "id": "w-mo21fy79-c6e76e507be3",
    "type": "brief",
    "status": "open",
    "title": "Attempt manual merge when sealing engine fails",
    "body": "The current clockwork 'sealing' engine attempts a fast-forward only rebase of work completed for a writ. If there are other changes that land, this means the engine will seize due to the conflict and the whole rig becomes stuck. We should make this process more robust as follows:\n\n- If the clockwork sealing engine fails, it should graft a new tail into the rig\n- The new tail should consist of two new engines:\n  - a quick engine, which instructs an anima to rebase the writ changes onto the new HEAD, or fail if it cannot do so unambiguously or without risk of error or loss from either change. The anima should output as its final message some structured content indicating if the merge was successful or not. This engine should fail if the anima indicates failure\n  - a new clockwork engine which runs after the quick engine, and uses same/similar logic as the default sealing engine -- attempt to push to main, rebasing (ff-only) if needed with a limited set of retries\n  - if this second clockwork engine cannot push due to conflicts, it should just fail and allow the rig to become stuck (i.e. only one attempt at manual merging via llm)",
    "codex": "nexus",
    "createdAt": "2026-04-16T22:15:42.309Z",
    "updatedAt": "2026-04-16T22:16:52.223Z"
  },
  {
    "id": "w-mo217smj-30b61eefedc5",
    "type": "brief",
    "status": "open",
    "title": "Disable 'piece' creation logic",
    "body": "Something in astrolabe or spider is creating 'piece' child writs underneath mandates prior to implementation. The 'implement-loop' engine has been disabled because it performed poorly, so these child 'piece' writs just sit around open. We should remove or disable the code which is creating them, so that each 'mandate' writ stands on its own. Also, it looks like the 'task' breakdown (XML content) is being stripped from specs before they are posted as mandates. We should also disable this logic, so that the task breakdown is present in the final spec.",
    "codex": "nexus",
    "createdAt": "2026-04-16T22:09:21.835Z",
    "updatedAt": "2026-04-16T22:16:57.499Z"
  },
  {
    "id": "w-mo204x9s-b7558b655423",
    "type": "piece",
    "status": "cancelled",
    "title": "Add decision-review engine tests for the new filter and fast-path behavior",
    "body": "<task id=\"t4\">\n    <name>Add decision-review engine tests for the new filter and fast-path behavior</name>\n    <files>packages/plugins/astrolabe/src/engines.test.ts</files>\n    <action>Inside the existing `describe('decision-review engine', ...)` block, add four new tests covering each D10 scenario: (a) a plan where every decision has `selected` pre-set and no scope items exist trips the fast-path and transitions directly to `writing` with no InputRequestDoc; (b) a plan with a mix of reviewable and pre-decided decisions plus scope items produces an InputRequestDoc whose `questions` and `answers` contain entries only for the reviewable decisions and the scope items — the pre-decided decisions have no corresponding entries; (c) a plan where every decision has `selected` pre-set but scope items exist **also trips the fast-path** under D6 (plan transitions to `writing`, no InputRequestDoc is emitted) — this assertion derives from the D6/D10 reconciliation note in the brief, not from the parenthetical in the D10 option text; (d) a round-trip reconcile test where an analyst-set `selected` on a decision that never reached the InputRequestDoc is preserved unchanged after the reconcile pass. Follow the existing arrange/act/assert conventions in the file (look at neighboring tests such as `blocks on first run with decisions and scope items` and `custom override clears stale selected (regression: dual-state bug)` for shape).</action>\n    <verify>pnpm -w --filter @shardworks/astrolabe test</verify>\n    <done>The engine test suite passes with four new tests added, each covering one D10 scenario; each new test name clearly describes its scenario; and the tests would fail against the pre-change engine code (confirming they exercise the new behavior, not an existing code path).</done>\n  </task>",
    "codex": "nexus",
    "parentId": "w-mo204x3w-db43f02a2b4e",
    "createdAt": "2026-04-16T21:39:08.272Z",
    "updatedAt": "2026-04-16T22:07:40.093Z",
    "resolvedAt": "2026-04-16T22:07:40.093Z"
  },
  {
    "id": "w-mo204x8b-cc1d0ea59cbf",
    "type": "piece",
    "status": "cancelled",
    "title": "Filter pre-decided decisions from the InputRequestDoc and extend the fast-path",
    "body": "<task id=\"t3\">\n    <name>Filter pre-decided decisions from the InputRequestDoc and extend the fast-path</name>\n    <files>packages/plugins/astrolabe/src/engines/decision-review.ts</files>\n    <action>In the first-pass branch (plan status `analyzing`), compute the set of decisions whose `selected` is not yet set, and build the InputRequestDoc's `questions` and `answers` maps from that set only — pre-decided decisions produce no `questions[id]` or `answers[id]` entry (D7). Scope items continue to be included in the InputRequestDoc when one is emitted. Extend the fast-path so that when no decisions are reviewable the plan transitions directly from `analyzing` to `writing` **regardless of scope count** (D6, scope-agnostic); in that case no InputRequestDoc is emitted and scope items are implicitly auto-accepted. Do NOT add any validation of the analyst-set `selected` (D8). Do NOT change `buildDecisionSummary` or `buildAnalysisTags` (D9). Do NOT change the reconcile-time invariant check — it already handles analyst-set `selected` correctly.</action>\n    <verify>pnpm -w --filter @shardworks/astrolabe typecheck</verify>\n    <done>The first pass excludes pre-decided decisions from the InputRequestDoc, the fast-path fires whenever no decisions are reviewable (irrespective of scope), the existing reconcile/invariant logic is untouched, and the package typechecks.</done>\n  </task>",
    "codex": "nexus",

content.content:
Usage: nsg writ [options] [command]

writ commands

Options:
  -h, --help              display help for command

Commands:
  show [options] [id]     Show full detail for a writ
  list [options]          List writs with optional filters
  edit [options] [id]     Edit a writ, updating its title, body, type, or codex
  complete [options]      Complete a writ, transitioning it from open to
                          completed
  fail [options]          Fail a writ, transitioning it from open or stuck to
                          failed
  cancel [options] [id]   Cancel a writ, transitioning it from new, open, or
                          stuck to cancelled
  publish [options] [id]  Publish a draft writ, transitioning it from new to
                          open
  link [options]          Link two writs with a typed relationship
  unlink [options]        Remove a link between two writs
  types                   List available writ types for this guild
  help [command]          display help for command

content.content:
1	The current clockwork 'sealing' engine attempts a fast-forward only rebase of work completed for a writ. If there are other changes that land, this means the engine will seize due to the conflict and the whole rig becomes stuck. We should make this process more robust as follows:
2	
3	- If the clockwork sealing engine fails, it should graft a new tail into the rig
4	- The new tail should consist of two new engines:
5	  - a quick engine, which instructs an anima to rebase the writ changes onto the new HEAD, or fail if it cannot do so unambiguously or without risk of error or loss from either change. The anima should output as its final message some structured content indicating if the merge was successful or not. This engine should fail if the anima indicates failure
6	  - a new clockwork engine which runs after the quick engine, and uses same/similar logic as the default sealing engine -- attempt to push to main, rebasing (ff-only) if needed with a limited set of retries
7	  - if this second clockwork engine cannot push due to conflicts, it should just fail and allow the rig to become stuck (i.e. only one attempt at manual merging via llm)

file.content:
The current clockwork 'sealing' engine attempts a fast-forward only rebase of work completed for a writ. If there are other changes that land, this means the engine will seize due to the conflict and the whole rig becomes stuck. We should make this process more robust as follows:

- If the clockwork sealing engine fails, it should graft a new tail into the rig
- The new tail should consist of two new engines:
  - a quick engine, which instructs an anima to rebase the writ changes onto the new HEAD, or fail if it cannot do so unambiguously or without risk of error or loss from either change. The anima should output as its final message some structured content indicating if the merge was successful or not. This engine should fail if the anima indicates failure
  - a new clockwork engine which runs after the quick engine, and uses same/similar logic as the default sealing engine -- attempt to push to main, rebasing (ff-only) if needed with a limited set of retries
  - if this second clockwork engine cannot push due to conflicts, it should just fail and allow the rig to become stuck (i.e. only one attempt at manual merging via llm)

content.content: 1	Something in astrolabe or spider is creating 'piece' child writs underneath mandates prior to implementation. The 'implement-loop' engine has been disabled because it performed poorly, so these child 'piece' writs just sit around open. We should remove or disable the code which is creating them, so that each 'mandate' writ stands on its own. Also, it looks like the 'task' breakdown (XML content) is being stripped from specs before they are posted as mandates. We should also disable this logic, so that the task breakdown is present in the final spec.

    "body": "# Teach analysts the razor + codify pre-fill defaults; engine fast-paths when nothing is reviewable

## Intent

Add codified policy to the Astrolabe sage instruction files (the razor's five criteria and three pre-fill defaults) and change the decision-review engine so pre-decided decisions are excluded from the InputRequestDoc and plans with nothing reviewable skip the patron-approval gate entirely. The goal is fewer rubber-stamp decisions reaching the patron.

## Rationale

The empirical baseline over 38 specs is a 3.7% override rate — analysts are surfacing many decisions the patron accepts unchanged. The razor (five criteria) gives analysts a sharp test for *what warrants surfacing*; the three codified defaults give them a disposition for *everything else*. Without the engine change, analysts could pre-fill all day and the patron-approval gate would still open — so the engine must honor the analyst's pre-decision by dropping those decisions from the InputRequestDoc and short-circuiting when nothing remains to review.

## Scope & Blast Radius

Changes are confined to `packages/plugins/astrolabe`. There are no cross-plugin changes, no type/schema changes, no rig-template changes, and no tool-registration changes.

Affected systems:

- **Two sage instruction markdown files** — parallel content additions: the new razor section, the new three-defaults section, a revised `selected` pre-fill rule, and an added Process-step note about "investigate, don't punt." Both files must end up with structurally equivalent decision-analysis guidance.
- **The decision-review engine's first pass** — a filter on `selected === undefined` governs both the InputRequestDoc membership and the fast-path condition.
- **The decision-review engine test suite** — new cov

## Intent

Add codified policy to the Astrolabe sage instruction files (the razor's five criteria and three pre-fill defaults) and change the decision-review engine so pre-decided decisions are excluded from the InputRequestDoc and plans with nothing reviewable skip the patron-approval gate entirely. The goal is fewer rubber-stamp decisions reaching the patron.

## Rationale

The empirical baseline over 38 specs is a 3.7% override rate — analysts are surfacing many decisions the patron accepts unchanged. The razor (five criteria) gives analysts a sharp test for *what warrants surfacing*; the three codified defaults give them a disposition for *everything else*. Without the engine change, analysts could pre-fill all day and the patron-approval gate would still open — so the engine must honor the analyst's pre-decision by dropping those decisions from the InputRequestDoc and short-circuiting when nothing remains to review.

## Scope & Blast Radius

Changes are confined to `packages/plugins/astrolabe`. There are no cross-plugin changes, no type/schema changes, no rig-template changes, and no tool-registration changes.

Affected systems:

- **Two sage instruction markdown files** — parallel content additions: the new razor section, the new three-defaults section, a revised `selected` pre-fill rule, and an added Process-step note about "investigate, don't punt." Both files must end up with structurally equivalent decision-analysis guidance.
- **The decision-review engine's first pass** — a filter on `selected === undefined` governs both the InputRequestDoc membership and the fast-path condition.
- **The decision-review engine test suite** — new coverage for the filter-and-fast-path scenarios.

Cross-cutting concerns to verify independently (the implementer must audit, not trust this list):

- **Duplicate decision-analysis language across `sage-reading-analyst.md` and `sage-analyst.md`.** Every razor/defaults change must appear in both files. Verify parity by diffing the two decision-analysis sections after editing; the only expected differences are file-local framing (heading levels, surrounding Process steps) — the razor content, three defaults, and pre-fill rule must read the same.
- **References to the old "always pre-fill `selected` with your recommendation" rule.** Grep both sage files for residual phrasing; any survivor is a bug. The new conditional rule must replace, not accompany, the old text.
- **Consumers of the `Decision.selected` field.** The engine's first pass, the reconcile pass, `buildDecisionSummary`, and the invariant check all read `selected`. The analyst-set-`selected` pathway must work through all four — verify the existing invariant check still holds when `selected` is analyst-set rather than patron-set.

**Explicit scope deferral (D11):** The post-change verification pass (dispatching the planner on 3–5 commissions, measuring override-rate vs. 3.7% baseline, measuring gate-shrinkage) is deferred to a follow-up commission. Do not attempt verification here beyond running the automated test suite.

## Decisions

| # | Decision | Answer | Rationale |
|---|----------|--------|-----------|
| D1 | Where should the razor and three defaults live in the sage files? | Two new subsections `#### The Razor` and `#### The Three Defaults` inside `### Decision Analysis`, placed just before the existing "Each decision needs:" field list; rewrite the `selected` field description to reference them. | Co-locates policy with the flow it governs; analysts get stable named vocabulary. |
| D2 | Exact phrasing of the new `selected` pre-fill rule | "If the decision matches any of the five razor criteria, leave `selected` unset. Otherwise, apply the three defaults, pick the answer, and pre-fill `selected` with your choice." | Explicitly names both cases to prevent habitual pre-fill. |
| D3 | Cross-reference razor to existing `analysis.category`/`observable`/`confidence`/`stakes`? | Present the razor as a standalone classification; analysis metadata remains a UX-filter aid and is not logically tied to razor-match. | Keeps the razor a sharp policy tool; no schema change. |
| D4 | How strongly to codify "uncertainty about a non-razor decision is a cue to investigate, not to surface"? | Name it in **both** the razor section (as the "investigate, don't punt" rule) **and** the numbered Process steps / decision-analysis procedure. | Target behavior change; dual placement raises salience. |
| D5 | Ordering and format for the five razor criteria | Use the brief's exact numbering and short names as headers/bullets; expand each with one sentence of definition and one short example question. | Stable numbering matches source material and future reference. |
| D6 | New fast-path condition in the engine's first pass | Fast-path when reviewable decisions is empty **regardless of scope** (scope items are implicitly auto-accepted in that case). | Patron-directed: skip the gate whenever the analyst has settled all decisions, even if scope items exist. |
| D7 | How to treat pre-decided decisions in the InputRequestDoc? | Skip entirely — produce no `questions[id]` entry and no `answers[id]` entry. Only reviewable decisions and scope items populate the doc. | Simpler; no spider schema change; reconcile works unchanged for untouched decisions. |
| D8 | Validate analyst-set `selected`? | No. Trust the analyst; rely on the existing reconcile-time invariant for well-formedness. | Matches existing engine style; reconcile-time invariant is the existing enforcement locus. |
| D9 | `buildDecisionSummary` rendering of auto-decided vs. patron-confirmed decisions | No change. Auto-decided and patron-confirmed `selected` entries render identically. | Spec-writer sees uniform `selected` semantics regardless of origin. |
| D10 | New test scenarios | Add **all** of: (a) fast-path when all decisions pre-decided and no scope; (b) mixed reviewable + pre-decided + scope; (c) pre-decided only with scope; (d) reconcile preserves analyst-set `selected` through the round trip. | Matches the existing test-file thoroughness bar. |
| D11 | Verification pass scope | Ship the code+docs change; defer verification to a follow-up commission. | Out-of-commission for this change; S4 is not executed here. |

**D6 ↔ D10 consistency note for the implementer:** D6 selected scope-agnostic fast-path. Therefore the D10 test scenario "pre-decided only with scope" must assert the fast-path behavior (plan transitions to `writing`, no InputRequestDoc emitted, no input-request block) rather than "only scope questions appear." The four D10 scenarios still each get their own test; only the expected behavior for scenario (c) derives from D6.

## Acceptance Signal

- `pnpm -w --filter @shardworks/astrolabe test` passes, with the decision-review engine describe block containing new tests covering each of the four D10 scenarios; at least one of these tests fails against the pre-change engine code (proving the test actually exercises the new behavior).
- `pnpm -w typecheck` passes.
- Both `packages/plugins/astrolabe/sage-reading-analyst.md` and `packages/plugins/astrolabe/sage-analyst.md` contain `#### The Razor` and `#### The Three Defaults` subsections inside `### Decision Analysis`, with the razor criteria presented in the brief's canonical numbering/format and the D2-worded pre-fill rule replacing the old unconditional rule. A diff of the decision-analysis subsections between the two files shows only file-specific framing differences.
- In both sage files, "investigate, don't punt" guidance appears in both the razor section and a numbered Process step.
- Grepping the repository for `pre-fill.*selected.*with.*recommendation` (case-insensitive) returns no residual matches in sage instruction files — the old unconditional phrasing is gone.
- A plan whose decisions all have `selected` pre-set (regardless of whether scope items exist) transitions directly from `analyzing` to `writing` on the first engine pass, and no input-request block is emitted.

## Existing Patterns

- **Fast-path short-circuit shape** — see the existing `decisions.length === 0 && scopeItems.length === 0` check in `packages/plugins/astrolabe/src/engines/decision-review.ts` (around line 143). The new fast-path is a generalization of this pattern.
- **Conditional-include precedent** — the existing first-pass logic that omits `answers[decision.id]` when `recommendation` is missing (same file, around lines 170–173) is the closest precedent for the new "skip entirely" behavior. The new filter extends the pattern to skip the question, not just the answer.
- **Invariant enforcement style** — the reconcile-time invariant check (same file, around lines 293–302) demonstrates the engine's throw-with-ID-listing style; no new invariant is introduced here but the existing one must continue to pass.
- **Test-file conventions** — `packages/plugins/astrolabe/src/engines.test.ts` already contains the patterns to follow: `completes immediately when plan has no decisions and no scope` (fast-path test shape), `blocks on first run with decisions and scope items` (first-pass InputRequestDoc assertions), `pre-fills answers for decisions with recommendations and scope items` (pre-fill mechanics), and `custom override clears stale selected (regression: dual-state bug)` (pre-set `selected` scenarios — this last one already simulates the "analyst pre-set `selected`" shape the new tests will need).
- **Decision-analysis section layout** — the current `### Decision Analysis` section in `packages/plugins/astrolabe/sage-reading-analyst.md` shows the heading nesting style (`###` section, `####` subsections) and the field-list style to match when adding `#### The Razor` and `#### The Three Defaults`.

## What NOT To Do

- Do NOT modify `packages/plugins/astrolabe/src/types.ts`. No schema changes to `Decision`, `ScopeItem`, `PlanDoc`, or any other type.
- Do NOT modify `packages/plugins/astrolabe/sage-writer.md` or `packages/plugins/astrolabe/sage-reader.md`. The writer's "`selected` means the patron chose a listed option" text is slightly stale under the new semantics, but it is explicitly out of scope — fix it in a separate commission.
- Do NOT add first-pass validation of analyst-set `selected` (e.g., checking the key exists in `options`). Trust the analyst per D8.
- Do NOT add markers, flags, or distinguishing prose to `buildDecisionSummary` to differentiate auto-decided vs. patron-confirmed decisions. They must render identically per D9.
- Do NOT refactor the decision-analysis language shared between `sage-reading-analyst.md` and `sage-analyst.md` into a shared include or canonical single source. Duplication is accepted for this commission.
- Do NOT modify rig templates (`two-phase-planning.ts`, `three-phase-planning.ts`), the `decisions-write` tool, the `astrolabe.ts` tool registration, or `buildAnalysisTags`.
- Do NOT dispatch the planner on commissions to measure override rates or gate shrinkage. Verification is deferred to a follow-up commission per D11.
- Do NOT cross-reference the razor criteria to the existing `analysis.category`/`observable`/`confidence`/`stakes` fields. The razor is presented as an independent classification per D3.",

  {
    "id": "w-mo1wagye-441cbcee1849",
    "type": "mandate",
    "status": "completed",
    "title": "Oculus click tree view",
    "body": "# Ratchet Clicks Oculus Page

## Intent

Ship an Oculus page that renders the Ratchet click forest as an interactive tree view with a persistent detail pane, lifecycle and link management, status/subtree filtering, and deep-linking. The page lives in the Ratchet plugin, uses the shared Oculus chrome, and matches the Clerk writs-page conventions except where click semantics diverge.

## Rationale

Clicks are the substrate's inquiry log, but until now the only views have been CLI (`click-tree` ASCII) and structured tool output. A browser view makes the forest navigable, puts lifecycle actions and links under direct manipulation, and closes the loop the docs already flag as "Future: Oculus Click View". The page also introduces the pattern for any future Ratchet-contributed pages — package publishing wiring, kit registration, companion tests — so subsequent pages inherit the convention.

## Scope & Blast Radius

Changes are confined to `packages/plugins/ratchet/`. There is **no cross-package blast radius** — no Oculus core changes, no Clerk changes, no schema changes.

- **Ratchet plugin source**: register the new page in `supportKit.pages`, extend the existing `click-tree` tool to accept a new output format, and add a plugin-level test asserting the page registration.
- **Ratchet page assets**: a new `pages/clicks/` directory containing `index.html` and companion `*.test.js` files.
- **Ratchet package manifest**: extend the published `files` array so the new directory ships with the npm package (Oculus resolves page directories from `node_modules` at runtime — unpublished assets resolve to 404, which is a post-install failure mode the implementer must preempt).
- **Ratchet test runner**: if the existing test glob does not pick up the new `pages/**/*.test.js` files, extend whichever script runs the test suite so the companion tests execute in CI. Verify by running the suite and confirming the new tests are discovered.
- **`click-tree` tool surface**: this commission adds a new output format option to the tool. The CLI default output must remain unchanged — existing callers must observe no behavioral difference. Verify by running the existing `click-tree` test suite and confirming it still passes without modification to the assertions.

Cross-cutting concerns to audit rather than enumerate:

- **Tool output contract**: `click-tree` currently returns a rendered ASCII string. Extending it with a JSON output path must not regress any current consumer. Audit by grep for uses of `click-tree` / `clickTree` across the monorepo and by running the full test suite.
- **Kit contribution shape**: the new `pages` entry in Ratchet's `supportKit` must match the `PageContribution` type consumed by Oculus. Confirm by reading the Oculus kit registration code and the Clerk precedent, and by running `pnpm -w typecheck`.
- **Published file footprint**: any new directory under `packages/plugins/ratchet/` that the page depends on at runtime must be listed in `package.json` `files` or an ancestor entry. Verify by inspecting the published tarball contents (`pnpm pack` and list the tarball) or at minimum by reading the `files` array.

## Decisions

| # | Decision | Default | Rationale |
|---|----------|---------|-----------|
| D1 | How does the page obtain the tree data? | Extend the `click-tree` tool with a JSON output format; the page consumes structured data from `/api/click/tree` rather than the rendered ASCII. | Patron override accepted the API expansion to keep CLI and web view consuming the same endpoint. |
| D2 | Where do the page assets live? | `packages/plugins/ratchet/pages/clicks/index.html` with companion `*.test.js` files in the same folder. | Mirrors the Clerk `pages/writs/` convention exactly. |
| D3 | How does Ratchet publish the page directory? | Extend `files` in `package.json` to include `"pages"` alongside `"dist"`. | Exactly mirrors Clerk; future-proofs additional Ratchet pages. |
| D4 | What is the detail-pane layout? | Persistent right-hand panel; the tree stays visible alongside the details. | Patron override preferring split layout over the writs-page full-page switch. |
| D5 | What does clicking a tree node do? | Click the node label → open detail in the right pane. Subtree scoping is a separate affordance (e.g. a focus button) on each node. | Matches the higher-information-first default; scoping remains accessible via the explicit focus control and via URL deep-link. |
| D6 | Status-filter semantic | Prune: non-matching nodes and their descendants are hidden entirely (matches CLI and API). | Matches `ratchet.tree()` and CLI conventions. |
| D7 | Default status-filter state | All four statuses selected by default. | Matches writs-page "All" default. |
| D8 | Default expand/collapse state | All nodes expanded by default. | Matches CLI output; scannable at realistic corpus size. |
| D9 | Tree-node status indicators | **CSS badges** (`.badge--active`, `.badge--warning`, `.badge--success`, `.badge--error`) — **not Unicode symbols**. | Patron override: use the shared Oculus badge vocabulary rather than the CLI's Unicode glyphs on tree nodes. (This intentionally diverges from S2's original Unicode framing.) |
| D10 | Terminal-click inline conclusion | Disclosure triangle (▸/▾) next to the goal; clicking expands the conclusion under the goal line. | Brief specifies "inline, collapsed by default, expandable". |
| D11 | Where do lifecycle actions live? | Detail pane only — user selects a node, then the pane exposes Park / Resume / Conclude / Drop. No row-level action menus. | Simpler; Conclude/Drop already need a text field that fits the pane. |
| D12 | Parent-click selector in the create form | Plain text input for parent ID (the server resolves short prefixes via `resolveId`). | Simplest; works for both root and child creation; pre-fill is additive. |
| D13 | Create-click form position | Toggleable inline card at the top of the tree view. | Mirrors the writs-page `#post-section`. |
| D14 | Link-type selector | `<select>` dropdown with the four fixed values (`related`, `commissioned`, `supersedes`, `depends-on`). | `linkType` is a closed enum — the dropdown makes invalid input impossible client-side. |
| D15 | Cross-substrate link navigation | Dispatch on target ID prefix: `c-…` opens the click detail in-page; `w-…` navigates to `/pages/writs/?writ=<id>`; anything else renders as plain text. | Pure-function dispatch based on the `w-`/`c-` convention. |
| D16 | Link-target display in detail pane | Show the target ID plus lazily-fetched metadata (click goal or writ title). | Patron override: more context than the writs-page id-only rendering. |
| D17 | Post-mutation refresh | Re-fetch the full tree and re-render after any mutation. | Simplest and always correct; matches "don't over-engineer for thousands". |
| D18 | Child ordering under a parent | `createdAt` ascending (oldest first). | Matches `ratchet.tree()` and CLI. |
| D19 | Deep-link by click ID | Support `?click=<id>` to auto-open the detail pane on page load. | Symmetric with writs page; cheap. |
| D20 | Status badge classes in the detail pane | `live → badge--active` (animated cyan), `parked → badge--warning` (yellow), `concluded → badge--success` (green), `dropped → badge--error` (red). | Reuses Oculus shared classes; semantics align. |
| D21 | Companion tests | Write `*.test.js` files alongside `index.html` covering tree-build, prune-by-status, subtree scoping, detail-pane rendering, and link prefix dispatch. Extend Ratchet's test script glob if the runner does not pick them up. | Matches the Clerk-page convention; keeps contribution bar uniform. |
| D22 | Children section in the detail pane | Status-count badge strip **plus** a table of first-generation children with clickable rows that navigate to child detail. | Mirrors the writs page. |
| D23 | Subtree-scope URL and breadcrumb | `?root=<id>` in the URL (via `pushState`); breadcrumb renders the path from forest root to the scoped root, each segment clickable to scope to that ancestor; a "Show all" entry returns to the full forest. | Deep-linkable, composable with `?click=<id>`, standard hierarchical affordance. |
| D24 | Detail-pane fields | Render every populated field: id, goal, status, parent link (if set), `createdAt`, `resolvedAt` (if set), `createdSessionId` (if set), `resolvedSessionId` (if set), conclusion (expandable, if terminal), links, children. | Brief says "all fields"; matches writs-page details grid. |
| D25 | Plugin-level registration test | Add a test in `ratchet.test.ts` asserting `supportKit.pages` contains `{ id: 'clicks', title: 'Clicks', dir: 'pages/clicks' }`. | Matches the Clerk precedent; locks the contract. |

## Acceptance Signal

- The monorepo builds and type-checks cleanly: `pnpm -w typecheck` and `pnpm -w build` succeed.
- The full test suite passes: `pnpm -w test`. This includes the new companion page tests (tree-build, prune-by-status, subtree scoping, detail-pane rendering, link dispatch) and the new `ratchet.test.ts` assertion for `supportKit.pages`.
- Running Oculus locally and navigating to `/pages/clicks/` shows a Clicks nav entry, renders the click forest as a tree, and lets the patron create, park, resume, conclude, drop, link, and unlink clicks end-to-end. Each mutation is reflected in the tree after refresh. The persistent detail pane updates when a node is selected.
- Deep-links behave: visiting `/pages/clicks/?click=<id>` opens that click's detail; `/pages/clicks/?root=<id>` scopes the tree to that subtree with a breadcrumb; both may be combined.
- Status-filter buttons prune the tree (non-matching nodes **and** their descendants are hidden). Default state has all four statuses selected and all nodes expanded.
- A click whose `targetId` starts with `w-` navigates to `/pages/writs/?writ=<id>`; a target starting with `c-` opens that click's detail in-page; anything else renders as plain text.
- The published Ratchet tarball contains the `pages/clicks/` directory. Verify with `pnpm --filter @shardworks/nexus-plugin-ratchet pack` and list contents, or inspect the `files` array in `package.json`.
- The existing `click-tree` CLI default output is unchanged — previously-passing assertions continue to pass without modification.

## Existing Patterns

- **`packages/plugins/clerk/pages/writs/index.html`** — the architectural template. Copy the IIFE layout, `api()` helper, error-display helpers, `?writ=<id>` deep-link pattern, confirm-section idiom for terminal actions with a required text input, copy-to-clipboard affordance, and the chrome markup at top-of-file. Diverge only where click semantics require (persistent side pane instead of view switch, select dropdown for link type, prefix-dispatch for cross-substrate targets, subtree scoping).
- **`packages/plugins/clerk/pages/writs/writs-hierarchy.test.js`** and **`writs-type-filter.test.js`** — the companion-test template. Follow the `node:test` + `assert/strict` + `FakeElement` DOM-shim pattern, re-declaring the pure functions under test (they are scoped inside the IIFE, not exported).
- **`packages/plugins/clerk/src/clerk.ts`** (the `pages:` entry in `supportKit`) — the kit-contribution precedent. Ratchet's entry mirrors it one-for-one.
- **`packages/plugins/clerk/src/clerk.test.ts`** (the `supportKit includes pages contribution for writs` test) — the precedent for the Ratchet-side assertion added per D25.
- **`packages/plugins/ratchet/src/tools/click-extract.ts`** — the in-repo precedent for a `format: 'md' | 'json'` tool parameter. Use its shape when extending `click-tree` per D1.
- **`packages/plugins/oculus/src/static/style.css`** — the shared badge classes (`.badge--active`, `.badge--warning`, `.badge--success`, `.badge--error`) and button / toolbar / filter-button styles. Do not introduce new CSS for behaviors the shared sheet already covers.
- **`packages/plugins/oculus/src/oculus.ts`** — the chrome-injection contract. Auto-injection only touches the root `index.html`; nested assets serve untouched. The new `pages/clicks/index.html` is the root and gets chrome automatically.

## What NOT To Do

- **Do not** add drag-and-drop reparenting, a graph view, bulk operations, or any other stretch goal from the `docs/architecture/apparatus/ratchet.md` "Future" section. The `click-reparent` tool is out of scope for this commission.
- **Do not** change the default CLI output of `click-tree`. The new JSON format is opt-in via the added parameter; the previous rendering must remain the default.
- **Do not** introduce a build step, bundler, framework, or JSDOM-based test harness for the page. Match the writs-page no-build convention.
- **Do not** implement Unicode status glyphs on tree nodes — D9 overrides the original brief/scope wording and selects CSS badges.
- **Do not** add lifecycle actions to tree-row hover menus or row-level shortcuts — D11 restricts actions to the detail pane.
- **Do not** broaden the click-link validator, add server-side target-existence checks, or refactor `resolveId` coverage on `click-link`. These are pre-existing concerns flagged in observations; they are out of scope here.
- **Do not** refactor the writs page or attempt to extract shared helpers between the two pages. Duplication is acceptable for this commission; shared abstractions are a follow-up.
- **Do not** modify unrelated docs beyond the scope's direct obligations. Doc drift noted in observations (O6–O9) is logged for a future pass.

"confidence": "medium",
          "stakes": "low"
        }
      },
      {
        "id": "D22",
        "scope": [
          "S2"
        ],
        "question": "Should writ-edit also support the --strict param like commission-post?",
        "context": "The brief only mentions --strict for commission-post. writ-edit has the same scanning behavior but the brief doesn't mention a strict flag for it.",
        "options": {
          "commission-post-only": "Only commission-post gets the strict param. writ-edit relies solely on the guild config mode.",
          "both-tools": "Add strict param to both commission-post and writ-edit for consistency.",
          "neither": "Drop strict from both tools. The config knob is sufficient."
        },
        "recommendation": "both-tools",
        "rationale": "Consistency — if a patron wants per-call strict checking on post, they'll want it on edit too. The brief's omission is likely incidental rather than intentional.",
        "selected": "both-tools",
        "analysis": {
          "category": "product",
          "observable": true,
          "confidence": "medium",
          "stakes": "low"
        }
      }
    ],
    "observations": "# Observations

## Brief/Code Discrepancies

### No `writ-post` tool exists
The brief references a `writ-post` tool as a distinct integration point. This tool does not exist in the codebase. The only writ-creation tool is `commission-post`. The brief's three integration points (commission-post CLI, writ-post tool, writ-edit tool) collapse to two tool-level surfaces: `commission-post` and `writ-edit`. The spec should treat `commission-post` as covering both the CLI and tool-callable creation paths.

### No CLI command file for commission-post
The brief references `packages/framework/cli/src/commands/commission-post.ts`. This file does not exist. The CLI dynamically generates Commander subcommands from plugin tool definitions via `buildToolCommand()` in `program.ts`. There is no per-tool CLI file to modify. The --strict flag must be added to the tool's Zod params, not to a CLI command file.

### D20 references non-existent scope S8
D20 was written with scope `[\"S8\", \"S2\"]` — S8 does not exist. This should be corrected to `[\"S1\", \"S2\"]` as the scanner function name relates to both the scanner module (S1) and its integration (S2).

## Refactoring Opportunities

### Tool handler has no caller context
The `ToolDefinition.handler` signature receives only validated params — no caller identity, no request metadata. This means there's no way for a handler to distinguish patron vs. anima calls. The current workaround (scanning in the tool handler = patron boundary) works because agents use `ClerkApi` directly, but it's fragile — if an agent ever calls tools through the Instrumentarium rather than the API, it would be scanned. A future commission could add an optional `context` parameter to the handler signature with caller metadata.

### HTTP tool server returns 500 for application errors
In `tool-server.ts`, all non-Zod errors from tool handlers produce HTTP 500. A thrown injection-block error is an application-level rejection, not a server error. A future improvement could distinguish application errors (4xx) from unexpected failures (5xx), e.g., by checking for specific error types or a status code property on the Error.

### ClerkApi.edit() request type inconsistency
`EditWritRequest` requires `id` as a field, while `PostCommissionRequest` doesn't include an `id` (it's generated internally). When adding `injectionScan` to both request types, the field semantics differ slightly — on post, it's always a fresh scan; on edit, it replaces whatever was there. This isn't a bug but is worth noting for the spec writer.

## Conventions Followed for Consistency

### console.warn for non-error output
The codebase uses `console.warn()` for non-fatal warnings (see `clerk.ts` kit registration warnings at lines 141, 154, and `program.ts` guild load failure at line 179). Using `console.warn()` for injection match warnings in tool handlers follows this established pattern, even though a structured logging system would be better.

### Test runner is native Node.js
All tests use `node:test` + `node:assert/strict`. No third-party test framework (jest, vitest, etc.). The scanner tests and integration tests must follow this convention. The test glob pattern is `src/**/*.test.ts`.

## Potential Risks

### Regex performance on adversarial input
The brief requires <10ms for 10kB body. Standard regex patterns should easily meet this, but pathological regex (catastrophic backtracking) on adversarial input could violate the constraint. The spec should require patterns to be tested against adversarial strings (e.g., long repeated characters that could trigger backtracking). Using non-capturing groups and avoiding nested quantifiers mitigates this.

### False positives on agent-generated briefs discussing injection
This very brief — which discusses prompt injection patterns extensively — would likely trigger multiple scanner patterns if scanned. The benign corpus fixtures must include content that *discusses* injection (meta-content) to prevent this. The brief explicitly calls this out, but it's worth emphasizing: the test fixtures ARE the regression guard.

### WritDoc injectionScan field visible to agents
The `injectionScan` field on WritDoc will be visible to any code reading writs — including agents via `writ-show`. This means an agent can see whether a writ was flagged. This is intentional (the brief says downstream tooling should display warnings) but worth noting: a sophisticated attacker who can read the writ could learn which patterns were detected and adjust their approach."
  }
]

1	{
2	  "id": "w-mo21fy79-c6e76e507be3",
3	  "codex": "nexus",
4	  "status": "reviewing",
5	  "createdAt": "2026-04-16T22:16:53.511Z",
6	  "updatedAt": "2026-04-16T22:27:30.386Z",
7	  "inventory": "# Codebase Inventory — Manual-merge Recovery Tail for Sealing Engine

## Scope and blast radius

The change modifies the clockwork **seal** engine's failure path so that, instead of throwing on rebase conflicts (which leaves the rig `stuck`), it grafts a recovery tail of two engines. The change touches the Spider plugin (engine implementations + registry), introduces a new loom role (likely in the Astrolabe plugin alongside the other `sage-*` roles), and leverages the existing grafting infrastructure. It does **not** modify Scriptorium, the Fabricator, or the rig orchestration core — all mechanisms needed (graft, graftTail, structured output parsing, role registration) already exist and are used by adjacent engines.

**Affected packages:**
- `packages/plugins/spider` — new engines, registry update, possibly new types/yields
- `packages/plugins/astrolabe` — new role definition + instructions file (if we follow the sage-* pattern; otherwise Spider could host the role)
- Consumers of seal in rig templates are unaffected — the new behavior is internal to how seal recovers from failure
- Docs: `docs/architecture/apparatus/spider.md`, `docs/architecture/apparatus/scriptorium.md` reference the current "sealing seizes" semantics and will need updating

**Cross-cutting consumers of the seal engine (rig templates that include it):**
- `packages/plugins/spider/src/spider.ts` — `STANDARD_TEMPLATE` mandate rig (inscription → … → seal)
- `packages/plugins/astrolabe/src/two-phase-planning.ts` — planning rig uses `seal` with `abandon: true`
- `packages/plugins/astrolabe/src/three-phase-planning.ts` — same pattern
- Any future rig template that terminates in `seal`

The grafted tail will run *between* seal and whatever downstream engines exist (via graftTail). For current usages, there are no engines downstream of `seal` — it's the terminal engine in every template. The change still needs to behave correctly in the graftTail=undefined case and with empty-downstream rigs.

---

## Key files and what matters

### The engine being modified

**`packages/plugins/spider/src/engines/seal.ts`** — The clockwork seal engine (current behavior).
- Takes `abandon?: boolean` given and `draft` upstream yield.
- Calls `scriptorium.seal({ codexName, sourceBranch: 'writ/<id>', ... })` or `scriptorium.abandonDraft()` when `abandon: true`.
- Returns `SealYields` (see types.ts).
- Lets any exception from scriptorium bubble out — the engine fails by throw, Spider marks engine `failed`, rig becomes `stuck`.
- This is the file that must change: on caught seal failure, it must return a successful result with `graft + graftTail` instead of letting the throw escape.

### The seal operation itself (read-only for this brief)

**`packages/plugins/codexes/src/scriptorium-core.ts`** — `seal()` implementation:
- Fetches remote, advances target branch to remote tip.
- Loop (up to `maxRetries`, default 3): checkout source, `git merge --ff-only target`. On failure, `git rebase target`; on rebase conflict, `git rebase --abort` and throw `"Sealing seized: rebase of <source> onto <target> produced conflicts."`
- On success: `git push origin <source>:<target>` then `abandonDraft()` unless `keepDraft: true`.
- **Critical invariant:** the draft worktree is **NOT** abandoned on the throw path — the worktree survives at `.nexus/worktrees/<codex>/<branch>` with the failed rebase state (rebase aborted, HEAD back at the source tip). The manual-merge anima can work in this worktree.

**`packages/plugins/codexes/src/types.ts`** — `ScriptoriumApi.seal()`, `SealRequest`, `SealResult`, `AbandonDraftRequest`. `SealResult = { ok: true, pushedSha, targetBranch }`. No typed failure variant; failure is always a throw.

### Grafting mechanics (Spider)

**`packages/plugins/spider/src/spider.ts`** —
- `tryRun` (lines ~1718-1745): after running a clockwork engine, duck-types the result for `graft` and `graftTail` keys (`runGraft`, `runGraftTail` locals). If present, queues a graft operation before marking the engine completed.
- `tryProcessGrafts` (lines ~1413-1473): validates graft (max engines, unique ids, no cycles, references exist), appends engines to `rig.engines`, then **patches existing engines' `upstream`**: any engine that depended on the grafting engine is rewritten to also depend on `graftTail`. This is how downstream engines end up waiting for the injected tail.
- Supports `collect(sessionId) → SpiderCollectResult { yields, graft?, graftTail? }` for quick engines returning a graft.
- **Registry (lines ~2013-2037):** `supportKit.engines` includes `draft`, `implement`, `implement-loop`, `piece-session`, `review`, `revise`, `seal`, `anima-session`. New engines must be added here.

**`packages/plugins/spider/src/types.ts`** —
```
SpiderEngineRunResult =
  | { status: 'completed'; yields: unknown; graft?: RigTemplateEngine[]; graftTail?: string }
  | { status: 'launched'; sessionId: string }
  | { status: 'blocked'; blockType: string; condition: unknown; message?: string };

SpiderCollectResult = { yields: unknown; graft?: RigTemplateEngine[]; graftTail?: string };
```
Key: **graft is only available on the `completed` variant** (or via `collect()` for quick engines). There is no "failed with graft" variant. This dictates the recovery pattern — seal must *swallow* the failure internally and return `completed` with the recovery graft, surfacing failure elsewhere if at all.

Also contains yields shapes: `DraftYields { path, sha }`, `SealYields { ok: boolean; ... }`, `ReviewYields { sessionId, passed, findings, mechanicalChecks }`, etc.

### Grafting patterns — templates to follow

**`packages/plugins/spider/src/engines/implement-loop.ts`** — Clockwork engine that grafts.
- Queries for children, builds a chain of `RigTemplateEngine[]` with sequential `upstream` dependencies.
- Returns `{ status: 'completed', yields: {...}, graft, graftTail: lastPieceEngineId } satisfies SpiderEngineRunResult`.
- Each grafted engine references the triggering engine's givens via `${writ}` and upstream yields via `${yields.draft.path}` templating — same templating as static rig templates.
- Model for the two-engine tail (manual-merge quick + retry clockwork).

**`packages/plugins/spider/src/engines/piece-session.ts`** — Quick engine with custom `collect()` returning `{ yields, graft, graftTail } satisfies SpiderCollectResult`. Uses `piece-session` engineId; demonstrates graft-from-collect pattern if we ever wanted the first tail engine (the anima) to decide whether to graft further work. Not likely needed here (brief says one attempt only).

### Quick-engine templates — structured output parsing

**`packages/plugins/spider/src/engines/review.ts`** — Quick engine with custom `collect()`:
```
async collect(sessionId: string): Promise<ReviewYields> {
  const session = await sessionsBook.get(sessionId);
  const findings = session?.output ?? '';
  const passed = /^###\s*Overall:\s*PASS/mi.test(findings);
  return { sessionId, passed, findings, mechanicalChecks };
}
```
This is the template for the manual-merge anima's structured output. The engine parses a marker from `session.output` (the anima's final message) and returns a typed flag. The brief requires the quick engine to **fail** if the anima indicates failure — in the review pattern, the engine does *not* fail on `passed=false`; downstream consumers branch on the yield. For this brief we'll need the engine to throw (or return a failure signal) if the anima's final message indicates the merge failed.

**`packages/plugins/spider/src/engines/anima-session.ts`** — Generic reusable quick engine.
- Takes `role`, `prompt`, `cwd`, optional `conversationId`, `writ` givens.
- Returns `{ status: 'launched', sessionId }` — no custom `collect()`, uses default collect (`sessionId`, `sessionStatus`, `output?`).
- Does **not** parse structured output or fail based on session content. For the manual-merge engine we need a purpose-built quick engine with a custom collect that parses success/failure and throws on failure.

**`packages/plugins/spider/src/engines/revise.ts`** — Another quick engine; useful as a template for wrapping an anima with custom prompt composition + collect.

### Role registration

**`packages/plugins/astrolabe/src/astrolabe.ts`** (lines ~262-283) — Sage roles registered via `supportKit.roles`:
```
roles: {
  'sage-reader':  { permissions: [...], strict: true, instructionsFile: 'sage-reader.md' },
  'sage-analyst': { permissions: [...], strict: true, instructionsFile: 'sage-analyst.md' },
  ...
}
```
Each role has an `instructionsFile` (resolved relative to the plugin's loom-roles dir). The new manual-merge role (e.g. `mender`, `sealer-merge`, `spider-merge` — name TBD) follows the same pattern. Location decision: Astrolabe hosts sage-* because the planning flow is astrolabe's domain; the sealing flow is Spider's domain, so the new role likely belongs in Spider's plugin (new roles dir). The Spider plugin already has `supportKit` infrastructure (see `packages/plugins/spider/src/spider.ts` lines 2013-2037).

**Permissions needed for a merge anima:** it must run git commands (rebase, merge, diff, status, log) in the existing draft worktree. Examine astrolabe roles for the `permissions` shape — typically a list of permitted tool/command prefixes.

### Rig and failure lifecycle

**`packages/plugins/fabricator/src/fabricator.ts`** — `EngineDesign`, `EngineRunResult`, `EngineRunContext`. EngineRunResult has `completed`/`launched`/`blocked` — **no `failed`**. Failure is expressed by throw.

**`packages/plugins/spider/src/spider.ts`** `failEngine()` — marks engine `failed`, cancels dependents, transitions rig to `stuck` (non-terminal; can be recovered by spawning a replacement rig).

**CDC cascade:** when a rig goes `stuck`, CDC handlers bubble the status to the owning writ (mandate/piece/etc.). For this brief, when the second clockwork engine in the grafted tail fails, the rig becomes stuck exactly as today — the recovery tail just shifts *where* the stuck transition happens.

---

## Adjacent patterns worth knowing

- **Clockwork engines returning grafts:** `implement-loop.ts` is the canonical example. Returns `status: 'completed'` with `yields`, `graft`, `graftTail`. Spider handles the graft in `tryRun` after the engine completes.
- **Engines that throw vs. engines that return `passed: false`:** The review engine distinguishes between "the engine completed and reports a failed review" (yield `passed: false`, engine itself succeeds) vs. "the engine couldn't run" (throw). For the manual-merge engine, the brief says "This engine should fail if the anima indicates failure" — meaning the engine itself should be `failed`, cascading the rig to `stuck`. This is different from review's pattern and needs explicit throw in collect or post-collect.
- **Templating in grafted givens:** `${writ}` and `${yields.<engineId>.<field>}` are resolved by Spider when the engine is scheduled. `implement-loop` uses both. Literal values (objects, primitives) can also be passed directly through givens — `implement-loop` does this with the piece WritDoc.
- **Draft worktree preservation:** scriptorium-core.ts on the throw path does NOT abandon the draft. The worktree path is the same one used by the engines upstream of seal — the draft yield `{ path, sha }` is still valid and the merge anima can use it.

---

## Existing context / prior art

- The brief itself (w-mo21fy79-c6e76e507be3) is the only directly-related writ; no parent, no children.
- No TODOs or scratch notes in `docs/known-gaps` or similar pointing at this area.
- The review engine (`review.ts`) is the clearest prior art for structured markdown output parsed from a session's final message.
- `implement-loop.ts` (recent addition — present in current HEAD) is the clearest prior art for clockwork-engine grafting with graftTail.

---

## Doc/code discrepancies noted

- **`docs/architecture/apparatus/spider.md`** — describes stuck semantics: "A stuck rig stays stuck for its lifetime; recovery happens by spawning a new rig." This remains true at the *rig* level, but will need an addendum: individual engines (e.g. seal) may now recover from specific failure modes by grafting a tail. The rig still goes stuck only if the tail also fails.
- **`docs/architecture/apparatus/scriptorium.md`** — describes sealing in a way that implies the caller can't recover from a seized seal. With this change, the sealing **engine** recovers (once), even though the scriptorium `seal()` method still throws unchanged. The docs conflate the two; worth clarifying the engine-vs-apparatus layering.
- **`packages/plugins/spider/src/types.ts`** — `SpiderEngineRunResult` has no "completed-with-failure" or "failed-with-graft" variant. The brief's intent ("if the sealing engine fails … graft a new tail") can't be literally encoded — the engine must catch the scriptorium failure and deliberately return `completed + graft` to reach the recovery path. This is a modeling subtlety worth calling out in the decisions.

358	  "observations": "# Observations — Manual-merge Recovery Tail for Sealing Engine

## Refactoring opportunities (out of scope)

- **Typed error discriminators on ScriptoriumApi.** The seal engine will sniff on the `"Sealing seized:"` message prefix to distinguish rebase-conflict from other failures (see D9). A cleaner long-term design is for Scriptorium to throw errors with a typed `code` field (`'rebase-conflict' | 'push-rejected' | 'config'` etc.) so callers can branch without string matching. Would affect Scriptorium's API surface and every caller that catches seal errors.

- **Unify `SpiderEngineRunResult` and `SpiderCollectResult`.** The two shapes carry the same graft fields (`graft`, `graftTail`) but differ in how the yields/status are conveyed. A shared `GraftDecision` mixin would remove the duplication and make future engines that want to graft from either run() or collect() easier to write.

- **Factor out the "anima-with-structured-output" pattern.** `review.ts`, the planned `manual-merge.ts`, and likely future engines all follow: summon anima → wait → parse final-message marker → yield typed result. A small helper (e.g. `parseMarker(session, /regex/)`) would centralize the marker parsing and make the convention explicit.

- **Role file co-location.** Astrolabe's roles live in a `loom-roles/` directory relative to the plugin; spider hasn't needed one yet. The introduction of the `mender` role should establish the same directory layout in `packages/plugins/spider/loom-roles/` and wire the loader to find it — this is a one-time convention.

## Suboptimal conventions followed for consistency

- **Clockwork engines signal success-with-delegation as `status: 'completed'`.** The seal engine after this change returns `completed` with `yields: { ok: false, grafted: true }` when the underlying seal throws — semantically the engine did not "complete" its work, it handed off to graft. This mirrors `implement-loop`'s pattern (which returns `completed` even when all real work happens in the grafted tail) but the mental model is worth calling out for future readers.

- **Structured markers in final messages.** Machine-parsing `^### Overall: PASS` from natural-language output is fragile. A more robust approach would be a reserved-channel final-message format (e.g. a trailing JSON or XML block) that animas are instructed to always emit. This is a guild-wide convention choice and outside this brief.

## Doc/code discrepancies

- **spider.md:** "A stuck rig stays stuck for its lifetime; recovery happens by spawning a new rig." Technically still true post-change (the rig only goes `stuck` if the grafted tail also fails), but the narrative is now misleading — engines *can* self-recover. Needs an addendum.

- **scriptorium.md:** Describes sealing in a way that implies "sealing seizes" means the whole operation is unrecoverable. The scriptorium primitive is still unrecoverable, but the Spider engine wrapping it is not. Worth clarifying the engine-vs-apparatus layering in both docs.

- **SpiderEngineRunResult** has no `failed-with-graft` variant. The brief reads "if the clockwork sealing engine fails, it should graft a new tail" — literally interpretable as a failed+graft variant. In practice we encode this as catch-and-complete (D1). Future engine designs that want real failure telemetry plus graft recovery will need to either extend the type or follow the same workaround.

## Potential bugs / risks noticed in adjacent code

- **Draft worktree survives the failed rebase in a potentially dirty state.** scriptorium-core's catch block runs `git rebase --abort`, but if that itself fails (edge case: interrupted rebase leaves the worktree in an ambiguous state), the worktree is left in an inconsistent state. The manual-merge anima walking in afterwards would see `.git/rebase-merge/` or similar and may be confused. Worth the anima's instructions defending against this (run `git rebase --abort || true; git reset --hard HEAD` first?).

- **No time-bound on manual merge session.** The mender anima has no deadline — a stuck anima could hold the rig open indefinitely. Animator has session-lifecycle controls but they're not wired to engine timeouts. Out of scope but worth knowing: a runaway merge session is a recoverable-but-uncomfortable failure mode.

- **Race: remote advances between manual-merge completion and retry-seal push.** The anima rebases onto `remote/main@N`, but by the time the retry engine pushes, main is at `N+1`. scriptorium.seal()'s internal retry loop handles this (fetch → ff-only → rebase retry, up to maxRetries=3). Under D11's recommendation this continues to work. But if maxRetries were constrained to 1, a transient race would spuriously fail. Relevant to D11's selection.

- **`graftTail` assumes the graft's last engine is the tail.** Spider's processGrafts trusts the caller's `graftTail` string. If seal sets `graftTail: 'seal-retry'` but the retry engine is mis-named or never appended, Spider's validation would catch it — but a silent typo elsewhere in the graft array would create a dangling dependency. Not a new risk, but worth a spot-check when testing.

- **Permissions drift.** The recommended `minimal-merge` permissions (D13) explicitly deny push. If an anima ever needs to stash or pop (e.g. to park unresolved conflicts), stash/pop are not on the initial allowlist. Permission additions may be needed once the role is exercised in practice; worth a soft-deny log rather than hard-deny to make expansion easier.
"
359	}
360

file.content:
{
  "id": "w-mo21fy79-c6e76e507be3",
  "codex": "nexus",
  "status": "reviewing",
  "createdAt": "2026-04-16T22:16:53.511Z",
  "updatedAt": "2026-04-16T22:27:30.386Z",
  "inventory": "# Codebase Inventory — Manual-merge Recovery Tail for Sealing Engine

## Scope and blast radius

The change modifies the clockwork **seal** engine's failure path so that, instead of throwing on rebase conflicts (which leaves the rig `stuck`), it grafts a recovery tail of two engines. The change touches the Spider plugin (engine implementations + registry), introduces a new loom role (likely in the Astrolabe plugin alongside the other `sage-*` roles), and leverages the existing grafting infrastructure. It does **not** modify Scriptorium, the Fabricator, or the rig orchestration core — all mechanisms needed (graft, graftTail, structured output parsing, role registration) already exist and are used by adjacent engines.

**Affected packages:**
- `packages/plugins/spider` — new engines, registry update, possibly new types/yields
- `packages/plugins/astrolabe` — new role definition + instructions file (if we follow the sage-* pattern; otherwise Spider could host the role)
- Consumers of seal in rig templates are unaffected — the new behavior is internal to how seal recovers from failure
- Docs: `docs/architecture/apparatus/spider.md`, `docs/architecture/apparatus/scriptorium.md` reference the current "sealing seizes" semantics and will need updating

**Cross-cutting consumers of the seal engine (rig templates that include it):**
- `packages/plugins/spider/src/spider.ts` — `STANDARD_TEMPLATE` mandate rig (inscription → … → seal)
- `packages/plugins/astrolabe/src/two-phase-planning.ts` — planning rig uses `seal` with `abandon: true`
- `packages/plugins/astrolabe/src/three-phase-planning.ts` — same pattern
- Any future rig template that terminates in `seal`

The grafted tail will run *between* seal and whatever downstream engines exist (via graftTail). For current usages, there are no engines downstream of `seal` — it's the terminal engine in every template. The change still needs to behave correctly in the graftTail=undefined case and with empty-downstream rigs.

---

## Key files and what matters

### The engine being modified

**`packages/plugins/spider/src/engines/seal.ts`** — The clockwork seal engine (current behavior).
- Takes `abandon?: boolean` given and `draft` upstream yield.
- Calls `scriptorium.seal({ codexName, sourceBranch: 'writ/<id>', ... })` or `scriptorium.abandonDraft()` when `abandon: true`.
- Returns `SealYields` (see types.ts).
- Lets any exception from scriptorium bubble out — the engine fails by throw, Spider marks engine `failed`, rig becomes `stuck`.
- This is the file that must change: on caught seal failure, it must return a successful result with `graft + graftTail` instead of letting the throw escape.

### The seal operation itself (read-only for this brief)

**`packages/plugins/codexes/src/scriptorium-core.ts`** — `seal()` implementation:
- Fetches remote, advances target branch to remote tip.
- Loop (up to `maxRetries`, default 3): checkout source, `git merge --ff-only target`. On failure, `git rebase target`; on rebase conflict, `git rebase --abort` and throw `"Sealing seized: rebase of <source> onto <target> produced conflicts."`
- On success: `git push origin <source>:<target>` then `abandonDraft()` unless `keepDraft: true`.
- **Critical invariant:** the draft worktree is **NOT** abandoned on the throw path — the worktree survives at `.nexus/worktrees/<codex>/<branch>` with the failed rebase state (rebase aborted, HEAD back at the source tip). The manual-merge anima can work in this worktree.

**`packages/plugins/codexes/src/types.ts`** — `ScriptoriumApi.seal()`, `SealRequest`, `SealResult`, `AbandonDraftRequest`. `SealResult = { ok: true, pushedSha, targetBranch }`. No typed failure variant; failure is always a throw.

### Grafting mechanics (Spider)

**`packages/plugins/spider/src/spider.ts`** —
- `tryRun` (lines ~1718-1745): after running a clockwork engine, duck-types the result for `graft` and `graftTail` keys (`runGraft`, `runGraftTail` locals). If present, queues a graft operation before marking the engine completed.
- `tryProcessGrafts` (lines ~1413-1473): validates graft (max engines, unique ids, no cycles, references exist), appends engines to `rig.engines`, then **patches existing engines' `upstream`**: any engine that depended on the grafting engine is rewritten to also depend on `graftTail`. This is how downstream engines end up waiting for the injected tail.
- Supports `collect(sessionId) → SpiderCollectResult { yields, graft?, graftTail? }` for quick engines returning a graft.
- **Registry (lines ~2013-2037):** `supportKit.engines` includes `draft`, `implement`, `implement-loop`, `piece-session`, `review`, `revise`, `seal`, `anima-session`. New engines must be added here.

**`packages/plugins/spider/src/types.ts`** —
```
SpiderEngineRunResult =
  | { status: 'completed'; yields: unknown; graft?: RigTemplateEngine[]; graftTail?: string }
  | { status: 'launched'; sessionId: string }
  | { status: 'blocked'; blockType: string; condition: unknown; message?: string };

SpiderCollectResult = { yields: unknown; graft?: RigTemplateEngine[]; graftTail?: string };
```
Key: **graft is only available on the `completed` variant** (or via `collect()` for quick engines). There is no "failed with graft" variant. This dictates the recovery pattern — seal must *swallow* the failure internally and return `completed` with the recovery graft, surfacing failure elsewhere if at all.

Also contains yields shapes: `DraftYields { path, sha }`, `SealYields { ok: boolean; ... }`, `ReviewYields { sessionId, passed, findings, mechanicalChecks }`, etc.

### Grafting patterns — templates to follow

**`packages/plugins/spider/src/engines/implement-loop.ts`** — Clockwork engine that grafts.
- Queries for children, builds a chain of `RigTemplateEngine[]` with sequential `upstream` dependencies.
- Returns `{ status: 'completed', yields: {...}, graft, graftTail: lastPieceEngineId } satisfies SpiderEngineRunResult`.
- Each grafted engine references the triggering engine's givens via `${writ}` and upstream yields via `${yields.draft.path}` templating — same templating as static rig templates.
- Model for the two-engine tail (manual-merge quick + retry clockwork).

**`packages/plugins/spider/src/engines/piece-session.ts`** — Quick engine with custom `collect()` returning `{ yields, graft, graftTail } satisfies SpiderCollectResult`. Uses `piece-session` engineId; demonstrates graft-from-collect pattern if we ever wanted the first tail engine (the anima) to decide whether to graft further work. Not likely needed here (brief says one attempt only).

### Quick-engine templates — structured output parsing

**`packages/plugins/spider/src/engines/review.ts`** — Quick engine with custom `collect()`:
```
async collect(sessionId: string): Promise<ReviewYields> {
  const session = await sessionsBook.get(sessionId);
  const findings = session?.output ?? '';
  const passed = /^###\s*Overall:\s*PASS/mi.test(findings);
  return { sessionId, passed, findings, mechanicalChecks };
}
```
This is the template for the manual-merge anima's structured output. The engine parses a marker from `session.output` (the anima's final message) and returns a typed flag. The brief requires the quick engine to **fail** if the anima indicates failure — in the review pattern, the engine does *not* fail on `passed=false`; downstream consumers branch on the yield. For this brief we'll need the engine to throw (or return a failure signal) if the anima's final message indicates the merge failed.

**`packages/plugins/spider/src/engines/anima-session.ts`** — Generic reusable quick engine.
- Takes `role`, `prompt`, `cwd`, optional `conversationId`, `writ` givens.
- Returns `{ status: 'launched', sessionId }` — no custom `collect()`, uses default collect (`sessionId`, `sessionStatus`, `output?`).
- Does **not** parse structured output or fail based on session content. For the manual-merge engine we need a purpose-built quick engine with a custom collect that parses success/failure and throws on failure.

**`packages/plugins/spider/src/engines/revise.ts`** — Another quick engine; useful as a template for wrapping an anima with custom prompt composition + collect.

### Role registration

**`packages/plugins/astrolabe/src/astrolabe.ts`** (lines ~262-283) — Sage roles registered via `supportKit.roles`:
```
roles: {
  'sage-reader':  { permissions: [...], strict: true, instructionsFile: 'sage-reader.md' },
  'sage-analyst': { permissions: [...], strict: true, instructionsFile: 'sage-analyst.md' },
  ...
}
```
Each role has an `instructionsFile` (resolved relative to the plugin's loom-roles dir). The new manual-merge role (e.g. `mender`, `sealer-merge`, `spider-merge` — name TBD) follows the same pattern. Location decision: Astrolabe hosts sage-* because the planning flow is astrolabe's domain; the sealing flow is Spider's domain, so the new role likely belongs in Spider's plugin (new roles dir). The Spider plugin already has `supportKit` infrastructure (see `packages/plugins/spider/src/spider.ts` lines 2013-2037).

**Permissions needed for a merge anima:** it must run git commands (rebase, merge, diff, status, log) in the existing draft worktree. Examine astrolabe roles for the `permissions` shape — typically a list of permitted tool/command prefixes.

### Rig and failure lifecycle

**`packages/plugins/fabricator/src/fabricator.ts`** — `EngineDesign`, `EngineRunResult`, `EngineRunContext`. EngineRunResult has `completed`/`launched`/`blocked` — **no `failed`**. Failure is expressed by throw.

**`packages/plugins/spider/src/spider.ts`** `failEngine()` — marks engine `failed`, cancels dependents, transitions rig to `stuck` (non-terminal; can be recovered by spawning a replacement rig).

**CDC cascade:** when a rig goes `stuck`, CDC handlers bubble the status to the owning writ (mandate/piece/etc.). For this brief, when the second clockwork engine in the grafted tail fails, the rig becomes stuck exactly as today — the recovery tail just shifts *where* the stuck transition happens.

---

## Adjacent patterns worth knowing

- **Clockwork engines returning grafts:** `implement-loop.ts` is the canonical example. Returns `status: 'completed'` with `yields`, `graft`, `graftTail`. Spider handles the graft in `tryRun` after the engine completes.
- **Engines that throw vs. engines that return `passed: false`:** The review engine distinguishes between "the engine completed and reports a failed review" (yield `passed: false`, engine itself succeeds) vs. "the engine couldn't run" (throw). For the manual-merge engine, the brief says "This engine should fail if the anima indicates failure" — meaning the engine itself should be `failed`, cascading the rig to `stuck`. This is different from review's pattern and needs explicit throw in collect or post-collect.
- **Templating in grafted givens:** `${writ}` and `${yields.<engineId>.<field>}` are resolved by Spider when the engine is scheduled. `implement-loop` uses both. Literal values (objects, primitives) can also be passed directly through givens — `implement-loop` does this with the piece WritDoc.
- **Draft worktree preservation:** scriptorium-core.ts on the throw path does NOT abandon the draft. The worktree path is the same one used by the engines upstream of seal — the draft yield `{ path, sha }` is still valid and the merge anima can use it.

---

## Existing context / prior art

- The brief itself (w-mo21fy79-c6e76e507be3) is the only directly-related writ; no parent, no children.
- No TODOs or scratch notes in `docs/known-gaps` or similar pointing at this area.
- The review engine (`review.ts`) is the clearest prior art for structured markdown output parsed from a session's final message.
- `implement-loop.ts` (recent addition — present in current HEAD) is the clearest prior art for clockwork-engine grafting with graftTail.

---

## Doc/code discrepancies noted

- **`docs/architecture/apparatus/spider.md`** — describes stuck semantics: "A stuck rig stays stuck for its lifetime; recovery happens by spawning a new rig." This remains true at the *rig* level, but will need an addendum: individual engines (e.g. seal) may now recover from specific failure modes by grafting a tail. The rig still goes stuck only if the tail also fails.
- **`docs/architecture/apparatus/scriptorium.md`** — describes sealing in a way that implies the caller can't recover from a seized seal. With this change, the sealing **engine** recovers (once), even though the scriptorium `seal()` method still throws unchanged. The docs conflate the two; worth clarifying the engine-vs-apparatus layering.
- **`packages/plugins/spider/src/types.ts`** — `SpiderEngineRunResult` has no "completed-with-failure" or "failed-with-graft" variant. This is a modeling subtlety worth calling out in the decisions.

  "observations": "# Observations — Manual-merge Recovery Tail for Sealing Engine

## Refactoring opportunities (out of scope)

- **Typed error discriminators on ScriptoriumApi.** The seal engine will sniff on the `"Sealing seized:"` message prefix to distinguish rebase-conflict from other failures (see D9). A cleaner long-term design is for Scriptorium to throw errors with a typed `code` field (`'rebase-conflict' | 'push-rejected' | 'config'` etc.) so callers can branch without string matching. Would affect Scriptorium's API surface and every caller that catches seal errors.

- **Unify `SpiderEngineRunResult` and `SpiderCollectResult`.** The two shapes carry the same graft fields (`graft`, `graftTail`) but differ in how the yields/status are conveyed. A shared `GraftDecision` mixin would remove the duplication and make future engines that want to graft from either run() or collect() easier to write.

- **Factor out the "anima-with-structured-output" pattern.** `review.ts`, the planned `manual-merge.ts`, and likely future engines all follow: summon anima → wait → parse final-message marker → yield typed result. A small helper (e.g. `parseMarker(session, /regex/)`) would centralize the marker parsing and make the convention explicit.

- **Role file co-location.** Astrolabe's roles live in a `loom-roles/` directory relative to the plugin; spider hasn't needed one yet. The introduction of the `mender` role should establish the same directory layout in `packages/plugins/spider/loom-roles/` and wire the loader to find it — this is a one-time convention.

## Suboptimal conventions followed for consistency

- **Clockwork engines signal success-with-delegation as `status: 'completed'`.** The seal engine after this change returns `completed` with `yields: { ok: false, grafted: true }` when the underlying seal throws — semantically the engine did not "complete" its work, it handed off to graft. This mirrors `implement-loop`'s pattern (which returns `completed` even when all real work happens in the grafted tail) but the mental model is worth calling out for future readers.

- **Structured markers in final messages.** Machine-parsing `^### Overall: PASS` from natural-language output is fragile. A more robust approach would be a reserved-channel final-message format (e.g. a trailing JSON or XML block) that animas are instructed to always emit. This is a guild-wide convention choice and outside this brief.

## Doc/code discrepancies

- **spider.md:** "A stuck rig stays stuck for its lifetime; recovery happens by spawning a new rig." Technically still true post-change (the rig only goes `stuck` if the grafted tail also fails), but the narrative is now misleading — engines *can* self-recover. Needs an addendum.

- **scriptorium.md:** Describes sealing in a way that implies "sealing seizes" means the whole operation is unrecoverable. The scriptorium primitive is still unrecoverable, but the Spider engine wrapping it is not. Worth clarifying the engine-vs-apparatus layering in both docs.

- **SpiderEngineRunResult** has no `failed-with-graft` variant. The brief reads "if the clockwork sealing engine fails, it should graft a new tail" — literally interpretable as a failed+graft variant. In practice we encode this as catch-and-complete (D1). Future engine designs that want real failure telemetry plus graft recovery will need to either extend the type or follow the same workaround.

## Potential bugs / risks noticed in adjacent code

- **Draft worktree survives the failed rebase in a potentially dirty state.** scriptorium-core's catch block runs `git rebase --abort`, but if that itself fails (edge case: interrupted rebase leaves the worktree in an ambiguous state), the worktree is left in an inconsistent state. The manual-merge anima walking in afterwards would see `.git/rebase-merge/` or similar and may be confused. Worth the anima's instructions defending against this (run `git rebase --abort || true; git reset --hard HEAD` first?).

- **No time-bound on manual merge session.** The mender anima has no deadline — a stuck anima could hold the rig open indefinitely. Animator has session-lifecycle controls but they're not wired to engine timeouts. Out of scope but worth knowing: a runaway merge session is a recoverable-but-uncomfortable failure mode.

- **Race: remote advances between manual-merge completion and retry-seal push.** The anima rebases onto `remote/main@N`, but by the time the retry engine pushes, main is at `N+1`. scriptorium.seal()'s internal retry loop handles this (fetch → ff-only → rebase retry, up to maxRetries=3). Under D11's recommendation this continues to work. But if maxRetries were constrained to 1, a transient race would spuriously fail. Relevant to D11's selection.

- **`graftTail` assumes the graft's last engine is the tail.** Spider's processGrafts trusts the caller's `graftTail` string. If seal sets `graftTail: 'seal-retry'` but the retry engine is mis-named or never appended, Spider's validation would catch it — but a silent typo elsewhere in the graft array would create a dangling dependency. Not a new risk, but worth a spot-check when testing.

- **Permissions drift.** The recommended `minimal-merge` permissions (D13) explicitly deny push. If an anima ever needs to stash or pop (e.g. to park unresolved conflicts), stash/pop are not on the initial allowlist. Permission additions may be needed once the role is exercised in practice; worth a soft-deny log rather than hard-deny to make expansion easier.
"
}
